source.title: Making working memory work: a computational model of learning in the prefrontal cortex and basal ganglia.

confidence_rationale: Foundational computational model (PBWM) of basal-ganglia-based dynamic gating of prefrontal working memory, with D1 (Go) and D2 (NoGo) pathways implementing opposing learning signals. This is the canonical model for an asymmetric gate: the D1/D2 balance shifts the enter threshold (easier to switch) vs the maintenance threshold (harder to dislodge). Directly relevant to MECH-266's Schmitt-trigger framing. Evidence direction is mixed because O'Reilly/Frank model implements asymmetry as opposing pathways rather than per-mode (enter, exit) pairs -- the architecture supports the general claim that gating thresholds are asymmetric, but does not specifically endorse the MECH-266 implementation where each mode has its own explicit (enter, exit) parameters. Confidence 0.60 reflects the architectural fit with caveat that the model does not distinguish between different operating modes.
failure_signatures: The PBWM D1/D2 opposition is a single-gate mechanism; it does not natively support per-mode asymmetry parameters. If the biological implementation of mode-specific asymmetry is via a shared D1/D2 gate whose tonic DA setting varies with context, MECH-266's per-mode parameter structure is not the right architectural parameterization.

The model was designed for 1-2-AX-style WM tasks, not for high-level operating-mode transitions. The transfer from 'WM item update gating' to 'operating mode transition' is a scale jump that the model does not directly address.

mapping.source_claim_statement: The prefrontal cortex and basal ganglia together implement a dynamic gating mechanism for controlling working-memory updating. D1-pathway neurons (Go) learn to gate updates in; D2-pathway neurons (NoGo) learn to suppress inappropriate updates. The actor-critic architecture solves temporal and structural credit-assignment jointly, enabling the PFC to learn when to update its representations.
mapping.ree_translation: MECH-266 posits asymmetric (enter, exit) thresholds per operating mode as an extension of SD-032a's mode register. The PBWM framework supports the general principle that the gate between stable-hold and flexible-update has asymmetric controllers (Go vs NoGo pathways). This is not the same as per-mode (enter, exit) pairs -- it is a single bidirectional gate controlled by two opposing signals. But the broader phenomenon -- that 'enter' and 'exit' dynamics are governed by different machinery -- is exactly what MECH-266 requires biologically. The translation cost is the per-mode specificity: PBWM has a single gate, whereas MECH-266 expects the asymmetry parameters to differ per mode.
mapping.mapping_caveat: PBWM is a computational model at the item-level WM gate. MECH-266 applies at the operating-mode level. The scale difference matters: item-level gating has specific D1/D2 circuits; mode-level gating in REE is implemented inside a single SalienceCoordinator without explicit D1/D2 analogues. The mechanism analogy is at the level of architectural principle ('asymmetric gate'), not specific implementation.
mapping.source_context: Computational model paper, O'Reilly and Frank, Neural Computation 2006. Foundational paper in the PBWM / frontostriatal-gating literature. Not empirical data.

confidence_components.notes: High source quality (foundational computational model). Mapping fidelity is moderate because PBWM's D1/D2 opposition is a different architecture than MECH-266's per-mode (enter, exit) pairs. Transfer risk is substantial: item-level WM gate to mode-level operating-state gate is a scale jump.
